preflight: Produce a concise preflight note that resolves the sample path if possible, captures only the minimum program context needed
for planning, and highlights a few high-value pivots.
Do not perform deep analysis, full obfuscation assessment, or final purpose conclusions.
Limit the handoff to path resolution, basic metadata, and at most a few worker pivots; do not include broad xref walkthroughs or large call-graph summaries.
planner: Produce a compact execution plan with prioritized work items, suggested worker-role assignments, and concrete evidence targets.
Include exactly one machine-readable JSON block delimited by `{{PLANNER_WORK_ITEMS_START}}` and `{{PLANNER_WORK_ITEMS_END}}`.
The JSON payload must be an array of objects with keys: `id`, `objective`, `recommended_roles`, and `evidence_targets`.
Base the plan on the original request, shared context, and any prior preflight output.
Every work item in the block must be executable with the currently available roles and tool capabilities for this run; omit out-of-scope dynamic or environment-dependent tasks.
Do not include sample-execution tasks unless shared context explicitly indicates sandboxed execution support is available for this run.
Do not perform fresh tool-driven analysis in this stage.
You may include a brief human-readable summary before or after the block, but do not omit the block.
workers: Produce a structured worker evidence bundle grouped by specialist or topic. Include artifacts and avoid unsupported conclusions.
Start with a compact sample-verification section when possible: `Validated sample path`, plus any obtainable hashes, size, timestamps, and key PE metadata fields.
For each major claim, use a re-checkable bundle that includes: the claim, the supporting artifacts, why those artifacts support it, and any inference/limits.
For code-behavior claims, artifacts should usually include function name/address, a short Ghidra decompiler or disassembly excerpt, and corroborating xref/call-site/import/string/rule evidence.
Representative artifacts for the important claims are usually sufficient; exhaustive per-call-site appendices are optional unless the task or review profile explicitly requires exact proof depth.
For validator-facing proof points, prefer verbatim or tool-faithful excerpts over paraphrase for the key artifacts.
When strings or imports materially support a claim, include raw string-table or import-table excerpts with the addresses/offsets or function names shown by the tool when available.
When a claim depends on argument passing, include a short call-site disassembly snippet showing the register/stack setup that supports the interpretation.
When xref analysis is used, summarize only the representative references that establish the claim; do not dump exhaustive xref walks or every reachable helper function.

If dynamic validation is proposed, include a concrete sandbox procedure with exact steps, monitoring points, representative logs when available, and rollback/safety controls; otherwise mark it as a recommendation rather than completed validation.
If sandbox support is unavailable for this run, explicitly mark runnable dynamic scripts and runtime logs as unavailable or out of scope rather than silently omitting them.
Do not enumerate startup/runtime scaffolding or trivial call-through wrappers individually; collapse them into a short omission note unless they directly gate meaningful behavior.
validators: Produce a review-only validation decision based on planner work items, worker outputs, and cited evidence already present.
Base the decision primarily on whether the original user request was adequately answered, not on perfect completion of every planner work item.
If some work items were impossible, unsupported, unsafe, or unnecessary, identify them as out-of-scope rather than treating them as automatic failure conditions.

If sandbox support is unavailable in shared context, missing runnable dynamic scripts, runtime logs, or detonation artifacts should normally be classified as out of scope or environment-limited rather than automatic rejection grounds.
Prefer narrowing or caveating claims over rejecting the whole run when representative evidence supports the core answer but some low-level corroborating snippets are missing.

Do not reject solely for missing exact VA-qualified disassembly, raw import-table lines, verbatim tool formatting, or minor metadata fields unless those omissions materially weaken a central claim or the current review profile requires exact proof depth.
Include exactly one machine-readable JSON block delimited by `{{VALIDATION_DECISION_START}}` and `{{VALIDATION_DECISION_END}}`.
The JSON object must include: `decision`, `signoff_count`, `required_signoffs`, `accepted_findings`, `rejected_findings`, `rejection_reasons`, `planner_fixes`, and `summary`.
When relevant, include `out_of_scope_work_items` to explain which planner items should not block acceptance.
Outside the block, keep any human-readable explanation concise and focused on accept/reject rationale.
reporter: Produce the final concise technical report using validated findings only, plus a short unknowns section.
Do not perform fresh analysis or new tool-driven discovery in this stage.
Center the report on user-authored logic, meaningful control-flow pivots, concrete capabilities, and supported obfuscation mechanisms rather than CRT/bootstrap helper functions.
